skill.name: design-to-code
skill.description: Converts Figma designs into React and TypeScript components through a guided coderio workflow with helper scripts, prompts, asset handling, and checkpoint files.
skill.summary: Guided Figma-to-React conversion with coderio prompts and workspace file helpers.
skill.author: MigoXLab

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high patterns. Review found no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection. Most alerts are false positives from license text, Markdown command examples, JavaScript template literals, and ordinary identifiers. Remaining risks are legitimate but elevated because the workflow handles a Figma token, performs network fetches, and writes workspace files.

medium_findings.title: Figma Token Passed as Command-Line Argument
medium_findings.description: The workflow instructs users to pass a Figma API token to the helper command, and the script reads it from process arguments before calling coderio Figma actions. This is legitimate for fetching design data, but command-line secrets can appear in shell history or process listings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token handling path is explicit in the user command and script arguments. I did not find evidence that the token is sent anywhere except the coderio Figma fetch helper, so this is a handling risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Generated Code Written Through Protocol-Derived Paths
medium_findings.description: The save-code command reads AI-generated code, resolves a component path from protocol data, and writes files under the project source tree. This is expected for the skill, but generated or malformed protocol data could influence where output is saved if the dependency path resolver does not constrain paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem write is clear, and the path is derived from protocol data. The final path validation is inside coderio workspaceManager, which is not visible in these files, so the exact traversal risk cannot be fully confirmed here.

low_findings.title: Documentation Shell Command Alerts Are Expected Usage
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md shell command detections are setup and workflow commands that the user runs intentionally, such as copying helper files, installing dependencies, and invoking node commands. They do not show hidden command execution by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown examples and explicit user workflow steps. No evidence found that these commands are executed automatically or covertly.

low_findings.title: License Keyword Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Apache license URLs and legal terms triggered network and weak-cryptography rules, but LICENSE.txt contains license text only. It has no executable code, no imports, and no credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited file is plain license prose. The hardcoded URLs point to Apache license pages and do not create network behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Naming False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography matches appear to come from ordinary words and identifiers in the design workflow. No hashing, encryption, or weak cryptographic API is used in the reviewed code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines define skill metadata or import and call coderio helper functions. No evidence found of MD5, DES, SHA-1, or any cryptographic operation in those lines.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM Database Alerts Are Identifier False Positives
low_findings.description: The critical SAM detections point to coderio prompt helper identifiers and logging. The reviewed script does not reference Windows system paths, registry data, or SAM database files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context is an imported prompt constant and a console.log call. No evidence found of filesystem paths or code that reads Windows credential databases.

low_findings.title: JavaScript Template Literals Misidentified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: Several script alerts are template literals used for error messages, prompts, and log output. They are not Ruby or shell backtick execution, and no child_process API appears in the reviewed helper script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are JavaScript string interpolation or Markdown code examples inside generated prompts. I did not find shell execution primitives in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command-Line Secret Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: A Figma token is supplied as a positional argument to the helper script. Users should prefer short-lived tokens and avoid pasting shared credentials into shell history.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and argument parsing are visible. The risk is credential exposure through local command history or process metadata, not confirmed malicious collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workspace File Writes from Generated Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper reads generated code and protocol data, then writes component output into src. This is core functionality, but it requires users to review generated paths and code before running builds.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file write behavior is explicit and expected for a design-to-code helper. The impact depends on user review and the unseen coderio path resolver.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose time translating visual designs into structured React components. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a coderio workflow that fetches Figma data, creates prompts, and saves generated UI files.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup for a local coderio helper script and script dependencies.
Fetches Figma document data and image assets when given a Figma URL and token.
Creates structure, props, and code prompts for iterative AI-assisted component generation.
Stores intermediate process files such as processed data, thumbnails, protocol files, and task lists.
Applies extracted props to protocol data and saves generated React component output.
Supports TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, and asset-aware React component workflows.
limitations: Requires Node.js, pnpm, coderio dependencies, and a valid Figma API token.
Generated code still needs human review, tests, and visual QA before production use.
The helper writes files in the local workspace and may overwrite generated outputs.
Accuracy depends on the Figma file quality, attached thumbnail, and model responses.

use_cases.title: Build a React screen from a Figma frame
use_cases.description: Use the guided phases to fetch design data, plan components, and generate React files for one screen.

use_cases.title: Create reusable component data from repeated UI patterns
use_cases.description: Extract component groups and props so repeated cards, lists, and sections can share structured data.

use_cases.title: Accelerate client design implementation
use_cases.description: Turn approved Figma mockups into a starting React codebase while keeping checkpoints for review.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Figma Conversion
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single design file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the design-to-code skill to help me convert this Figma file into a React and TypeScript project. Walk me through setup, fetch the design data, and identify the first checkpoint I should review.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Component Structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a component plan after fetching Figma data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the generated structure prompt and attached thumbnail to produce a clear component hierarchy. Name components from visible content, group repeated elements, and explain any uncertain layout choices.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Props for Repeated Components
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for cards, rows, or lists.
prompt_templates.prompt: For the selected component, extract props and state data from the thumbnail and Figma content. Keep text exact, preserve asset references, and flag missing or ambiguous data.

prompt_templates.title: Generate and Review Production UI
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced final code generation and quality review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate the React component for this task index, then review the result for visual fidelity, responsive layout, asset imports, accessibility, and risky generated code before saving it.

output_examples.input: A Figma landing page with hero, feature cards, testimonials, and footer.
output_examples.output: A phased implementation plan, a component hierarchy, extracted props for repeated sections, and saved React component files ready for review.

output_examples.input: A dashboard frame with repeated metric cards and a data table.
output_examples.output: A component task list, prop sets for repeated cards and rows, and generated TypeScript components using local assets.

output_examples.input: A partially generated UI that does not match the thumbnail.
output_examples.output: A checkpoint review that identifies missing assets, incorrect spacing, weak component names, and the next prompt to rerun.

best_practices: Use a short-lived Figma token and clear shell history after running fetch commands.
Review each checkpoint file before moving to the next phase.
Run formatting, linting, tests, and visual comparison before merging generated code.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated code without reviewing imports, paths, and side effects.
Do not skip the thumbnail attachment when asking the model for structure or props.
Do not treat the generated component hierarchy as final if the Figma frame has ambiguous grouping.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Figma token?
faq.answer: Yes. The fetch phase needs a Figma API token to read the design file and download assets.

faq.question: Can it create a complete production app automatically?
faq.answer: It creates a strong React starting point, but developers must review code, run tests, and finish integration.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What files does the helper create?
faq.answer: It creates process files, prompt files, thumbnails, task lists, and generated component files in the workspace.

faq.question: Is the security risk low?
faq.answer: The reviewed risk is medium because the workflow uses scripts, network access, tokens, and workspace file writes.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Use limited tokens, review generated files, keep checkpoints, and run normal code quality checks before merging.
